speaker: interviewee
text: From a industrial area. It wasn't by the big bridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it wasn't right in the center. This is tape number five, a continuation of the Aigner family. We are now talking to Eva Aigner, tape number five. You said there was one more thing that you wanted to talk about.

speaker: interviewee
text: One more thing I wanted to add to all this, that after Hungary, I was liberated from the Germans by the Russian. Of course, communism set in. And my hus-- my husband and I-- of course, we didn't know each other, then-- lived under communism for quite a few years, until 1956. And somehow, all this antisemitism in Hungary never left. I don't know if my husband mentioned it in his tape, that what made our decision, finally, to come away from Hungary and to this country. One day, during the revolution, we were standing in the bread line. And two people were standing in front of us, talking, and they said, let's get rid of the communists first. Then we get the leftover Jews. And at that moment, we looked at each other, with my husband, and we said, we're not going through this again. We don't want to bring children into this country. We want to leave. And that's when we escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you escape, actually? He briefly told us. If you want to give us a little--

speaker: interviewee
text: The way we escaped, we took the train to one of the border towns, and it was winter time, very cold. And we covered ourselves with sheets to blend into the snow, to get through the border line through-- toward Austria. And it was a very difficult time, because the Russians [INAUDIBLE] spot, and they were shooting flares up in the sky. But we were fortunate, after spending a whole night out in the snow, not knowing if we are really in Austria or in Hungary. Turn out to be we were in Austria, and by the villagers we were taken into the town and given quarters. And that's how we arrived to Austria. And from there, we oriented to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went with-- besides you and your husband, who went?

speaker: interviewee
text: We brought my husband's father and stepmother along. It was the four of us. And my mother-in-law just could hardly manage the snow. We finally set her in a little suitcase, and my husband took out his belt and pulled her through, like on a sled, on this suitcase, over the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your own mother, who-- you brought her to this country? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother couldn't come with us at the time, because my sister, at the time of the war and after the war, she contracted tuberculosis. And she was very sick for quite a few years. And just in the time of the revolution, she got pneumonia. She was very, very ill, and my mother wouldn't leave her. She wouldn't leave at the time, and she was so sick. By the time she was better, the borders were closed, and the rest of the family couldn't come. So on legal ways, we brought my mother out in 1968, and then she lived with us in Portland and she died here in Portland at age 84.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister stayed back in Hungary and raised a family over there and, unfortunately, she died at age 51 in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you still have family left in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have my niece and nephew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what we're going to do now is, first, I want to thank you for taking the time to come out and tell us, although I know it was not as long as your husband's, but-- because you were so young. What we're going to do is bring your husband back and bring in your two children, and we're going to talk to all four of you for a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. the beginning of our tape, when the grandchildren were here. But Mr. and Mrs. Aigner, now that-- standing behind you are your two children, Sue and Robert. Now that you're together, and it's 50 years, just about, since the war is over-- I asked you earlier-- but now that you've told me your stories, is there anything else that you feel that you want to say that you would hope for your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I feel that, the first few years, even after we came to the United States, we had a hard time to get over our fear. It was very difficult. It took a few years until we could openly admit we are Jews, because this fear was in us all the time. Is this ever going to happen with us again? And what I would like to pass on to my children now, that we got over this fear, and we have to talk about the Holocaust, for the simple reason so the future generation know what happened, and this could not happen again. And it's up to them to carry it on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Aigner, do you have anything else you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, basically, I can second this, what Eva told. But I-- the only thing I can add to it, I can thank my son that he instigated me to do this, and it helped me overcome with all the tragedy we went through. And I will go on lecturing and talking to people about the Holocaust as long as I can, so people learn, first-hand. Because not too many of us are left, and most of us are older. I can think that, as a 15-year-old, I'm one of the-- some of the youngest ones who are still surviving and living. And so, I will go on and tell what happened to us so it never happens again, hopefully, in this world. Though, it's going on, these religious indifferences in the world. But I hope that one day it will end. We will be peaceful, all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sue and Robert, being second-generation, I'm sure has-- has been difficult and-- at times for you. Are there any things that you'd like to talk about, about your parents, about growing up as second-generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can remember-- I can remember growing up with the fear, of a feeling of needing to hide things, of, you know, not being able to be open because of this unknown fear of someone-- someone's out there to get you. They're not going to be fair to you because of what you were born to be, what your heritage is. And so, this experience in the last year or so, of my parents being able to talk about it, has, I think, been a real cleansing thing, a real helpful thing for the whole family. And I don't know if my parents know that-- just the pride that I feel. I mean, it was a horrible thing to have happen to them, an awful, horrific, thing, but I have a lot of pride in how they've survived. Not just that they survived, but that they've not let it stand in their way to creating a decent life for the family. And-- and they've been able to carry on and make what good of it that they-- that they could. And I think coming out and being able to talk to people, and, you know, people that have been their close friends for years and haven't known about it, to come out and be able to talk about these things now, I see it really changing things for my parents and for all of us, at this point in our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Robert?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think one of the things I want to mention is that, you know, for such a long time, it was-- you know, growing up, it was just what we grew up with. It was normal for us. It wasn't really something that we considered as out of the ordinary. And, for me, anyway, it wasn't until I moved to New York and was in a more vibrant, Jewish community than Portland, and-- and I started striking up some interest in the topic, and read particular books-- one, which was prominent, was which I'm sure you're familiar with, and, you know, it was just a really amazing book for me to read because it was-- it was like seeing, verbatim, conversations I've had with my parents. And-- and it dawned on me at that point that, you know, this is a common experience, and it was the first time I really thought that it had some impact beyond their own personal experiences. So, I mean, I guess to other second-generation survivors, I'd want to encourage people to investigate it more for themselves. And, you know, I also have a lot of pride for my parents and what they've done, and what they're doing now, speaking to churches and organizations and schools. And, you know, what it's done for us as a family has [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] provided us an opportunity to understand ourselves better. So yeah, I'm really glad to see them expressing it, finally.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I think that-- I think that we've covered all of the territory. I want to thank you all for staying up, for [LAUGHING] being here late, for coming in from San Francisco, and we're going to get to the photographs next. Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my grandma, Jenny Kohn, on the left, and her mother. The picture was taken 1915, approximately. My grandma, on the left, was born in Czechoslovakia-- I don't know the town's name-- and she died in Auschwitz in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city-- do you know what city and country this photograph was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nove Zamky, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your great-grandmother, did she survive? Or did she die during the war? What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: My great-grandmother died before I was born. She-- I never knew her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know your great-grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm sorry. Don't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, next picture. --Ghettos and then the list of the camps, whereas, with other people, it's not [INAUDIBLE] to experience that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know somebody who [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Hey, this thing's rolling. OK, and we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rolling, dad.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was saved by my sister from the ghetto, just like the previous one. And this is the Kohn family, my grandmother and my grandfather on the first row. And in the middle is my mother and my sister, Elizabeth. On the left is my uncle, Danny, who moved to Israel before--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a million things go through my mind. I just want to ask, like, a thousand questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the Kohn family in Nove Zamky, grandmother and grandfather, Jenny Kohn and Joseph Kohn. In the middle is my mother with my older sister, Elizabeth, and she was about two years old. This picture must have been taken about '30 or '31, 1930 or 1931. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The uncles on top is Danny, who moved to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he on the left or the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's on the left. He's not showing over there. And Mathias with the hat on, and Bela, with a hat on, and my aunt, Clara, and my uncle, Alex Sandor. They all, except my sister, died in Auschwitz in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell us where Nove Zamky is, or where it was when this photograph was taken, in what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Czechoslovakia, Nove Zamky. They all were living over there, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] only my mother, at that time, was the first one with the first grandchild.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, next picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, again, a family picture of my little sister on the left side, first row.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- Marika. Her name was Marika Aigner, and she was born in Czechoslovakia, in Nove Zamky, in 1935. My father, who born in [? Chyse, ?] Czechoslovakia in 1900. The third one is my uncle, Alex, who born in [INAUDIBLE], and the rest of them is uncles and aunts. My mother is behind my grandma, above grandma.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- do you know approximately when this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- '38 or '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the full year, what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And of the people in this photograph, how many survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: The little girl on the left-hand side-- no, my father, next to the little girl, on the left-hand side, with the cap on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is the only survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the only survivor on this picture, yes, who lived with us in Portland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Next picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is about my mother. This picture was taken in Csepel, in the ghetto. And this, and the following three pictures, survived the war at the developer, as I told in my story. We made it in the ghetto, and in the-- in the last week, the following week, we were taken away. Her name is-- born name is Kohn, Anna, and was [? Julius-- ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mrs. Aigner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- approximately what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] June, about the 20th of June.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Next picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is second-- the second picture which survived at the photographer, that were made in the ghetto of my mother, my two sisters, and myself, wearing the yellow star, in 1944, June. Left is my little sister, Marika, my mother, Anna, myself, and my older sister, Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And exactly what city and country was this photographed in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Csepel, in 1944, June, in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the third picture which survived the-- the war at the photographer. This is my sister and myself, on the right-hand side, wearing the yellow star. My sister's yellow star doesn't show, because it's under her braid, and on the dress. My sister, Elizabeth Aigner, born in the Czechoslovakia, Nove Zamky, 1928. I, myself, born in Czechoslovakia, Nove Zamky, 1929, and the date of the photo is June 20 or so, in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city and country was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, it's made in Hungary, Csepel, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're rolling

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the fourth picture which survived. It was made in the ghetto, in Csepel, in 1944, June. It is made up of my little sister, who was nine years old at the time, Mari-- Marika Aigner, born in 1935, and myself again, wearing the yellow star, and my older sister, Elizabeth Aigner, in the ghetto, 1944, June. My little sister died in Auschwitz with my mother. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This postcard we ordered to write from Auschwitz, to somebody who left-- we left behind. I addressed this postcard to [INAUDIBLE], to Csepel, [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know the number. Anyway, this postcard, we were ordered to write to somebody, and I wrote this Gentile friend to pass it on to my father, who was, at that time, in a forced labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944, July 14. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] About a week later, we arrived to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bastille day.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a postcard I sent from Auschwitz on 1944, June 14th. We were ordered to sign-- write a letter back home to calm the nerves, and I wrote to this Gentile friend, [NON-ENGLISH]-- uncle, like if it would be my uncle. The rest of it I can't-- I just will translate it, because it's in German. I meant to notify my father. I asked Mr. [? Zorinast ?] to let my father know that I am with my friends, and I'm OK. Letting him know can-- letting them know candidly that I'm not with my mother and my little sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken six months after I was liberated. I was still wearing the American army uniform where I got in the-- from the liberators. And this was after I went through the field hospital for three weeks, and at home, I was put into a children's san-- sanatorium for another month. This is, not really, naturally gained back my weight. My system couldn't work out the water, and that's why-- that's why I looked so bloated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photograph was taken back in Csepel, Budapest, outskirt of Budapest, Hungary, in 1945, October. As I say, six months after liberation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Csepel, Hungary, with my father and myself, 1946, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] end of 1946. I was a lot healthier, and I was back to work learning my trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what am I supposed to look at, this or this?

speaker: interviewer
text: The article.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our wedding picture, which was made May 9th, 1956, after long engagement, which was 59 days. We got married 1956, and it wasn't a religious wedding, just in front of the justice of the peace, because it was under communist ruling. This is Eva Aigner and Leslie Aigner.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I think we're finished.
